coredata.dc:title: Design and In-silico study of bioimaging fluorescence Graphene quantum dot-Bovine serum albumin complex synthesized by diimide-activated amidation
coredata.dc:creator: Patel, Vimal
Shah, Jigar
Gupta, Ajay Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dot possesses advantageous characteristics like tunable fluorescence, nanometer size, low cytotoxicity, high biocompatibility enabling them as an ideal material for fluorescence bio-imaging. It exhibits a unique characteristic of DNA cleavage activity enhancer, gene/drug carrier, and anticancer targeting applications. In this article, we discussed the preparation of graphene quantum dot through the bottom-up method. Carbodiimide-activated amidation reactions were used for the functionalization of graphene quantum dot with Bovine Serum Albumin. Fluorescence spectroscopy data showed that the graphene quantum dot has size-dependent fluorescence emission. TEM and AFM studies showed that the size of graphene quantum dot was around 20 nm with narrow size distribution. Carbodiimide-activated amidation conjugation was successful in binding the protein onto graphene quantum dot and these conjugates were characterized by DLS, FTIR, fluorescence spectroscopy, and agarose gel electrophoresis. We also studied the structural-based in-silico molecular dynamic simulation by AutoDock, PyRx, and Discovery Studio Visualizer. Based on the virtual screening analysis and higher negative energy incorporation, it is observed that graphene quantum dot conjugated with bovine serum albumin quickly and formed is highly stable complex, which makes them a potential candidate for future applications in the field of bio-imaging, bio-sensing, gene/drug delivery, and tumor theragnostic.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Graphene quantum dot
Bovine serum albumin
Carbodiimide-activated amidation
MD simulation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement and evaluation of fluorescence emission from GQD nano-crystals
Morphological characterization and evaluation of GQD by AFM and TEM
Measurement of particle size distribution of GQD and its BSA complex in solutions
Identification of functional groups present in GQD-BSA complex by infrared spectroscopy
Evaluation of GQD-BSA complex based on fluorescence emission
Identification and separation of protein/macromolecule by 2D-electrophoretic assay

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge Dr. Alex George; Executive Vice President, R&D center, Dr. Reddy, Hyderabad. And Dr. Vijay Thiruvenkatam; Faculty of Biological Engineering and Physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Gandhinagar, Gujarat for their unconditional technical support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GQD: (a) UV-spectroscopy of GQD; (b) Fluorescence image of GQD excited at 360 nm blue UV light; (c) Spectro-fluorescence overlay 3D graph.
Fluorescence calibration plot of GQD at a concentration range of 10-100 mg/mL in 0.1 N NaOH.
AFM images of GQD; (a) 2D representation of AFM; (b) 3D representation of AFM; (c) Size distribution histography.
TEM image of GQD derived from citric acid.
DLS images of GQD and BSA complex: (a) Citric acid derived GQD; (b) GQD-BSA complex.
Characterization of GQD-BSA complex: (a) FT-IR overlay; (b) Fluorescence emission overlay.
Protein quantification in GQD-BSA complex: (a, b) 2D-agarose gel electrophoresis images.
Binding pocket of GQD on BSA protein.
Various interactions of the GQD with active site residues of the BSA protein: (a) 3D representation of the interactions; (b) 2D representation of the interactions.
Summary of binding affinities of different docked conformations/orientations of GQD-BSA complex generated by AutoDock vina in PyRx.
Details of interactions of the GQD with active sites residue (amino acid) of BSA protein.

head.ce:title: Design and In-silico study of bioimaging fluorescence Graphene quantum dot-Bovine serum albumin complex synthesized by diimide-activated amidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, Institute of Pharmacy, Nirma University, Ahmedabad, Gujarat, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutics, Institute of Pharmacy, Nirma University, Ahmedabad, Gujarat, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Research and Development, Ganpat University, Mehsana, Gujarat, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Research and Development, Ganpat University, Mehsana, Gujarat, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Pharmaceutics, Institute of Pharmacy, Nirma University, Ahmedabad, Gujarat, 382481, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: GQD tethered BSA complex by carbodiimide amidation and characterization.

Graphene quantum dot possesses advantageous characteristics like tunable fluorescence, nanometer size, low cytotoxicity, high biocompatibility enabling them as an ideal material for fluorescence bio-imaging. It exhibits a unique characteristic of DNA cleavage activity enhancer, gene/drug carrier, and anticancer targeting applications. In this article, we discussed the preparation of graphene quantum dot through the bottom-up method. Carbodiimide-activated amidation reactions were used for the functionalization of graphene quantum dot with Bovine Serum Albumin. Fluorescence spectroscopy data showed that the graphene quantum dot has size-dependent fluorescence emission. TEM and AFM studies showed that the size of graphene quantum dot was around 20 nm with narrow size distribution. Carbodiimide-activated amidation conjugation was successful in binding the protein onto graphene quantum dot and these conjugates were characterized by DLS, FTIR, fluorescence spectroscopy, and agarose gel electrophoresis. We also studied the structural-based in-silico molecular dynamic simulation by AutoDock, PyRx, and Discovery Studio Visualizer. Based on the virtual screening analysis and higher negative energy incorporation, it is observed that graphene quantum dot conjugated with bovine serum albumin quickly and formed is highly stable complex, which makes them a potential candidate for future applications in the field of bio-imaging, bio-sensing, gene/drug delivery, and tumor theragnostic.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomedicines and their interventions in cell biology have provide new approaches in biological assays and clinical diagnosis. Imaging of mammalian tissues and structures facilitate better diagnosis and management of the lethal diseases. Semiconductor metallic quantum dots are used in many fields of medicine and bio-imaging like long-term and real time imaging of several normal and malignant cells in-vitro and in-vivo, tumor taxonomy and therapeutic tumor targeting, etc. Still, potential side effects of these nanomedicines are a significant concern, prompting for exploring suitable alternatives with improved biocompatibility and toxicity (Song et al., 2015; Hill and Galan, 2017). Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) have made noticeable progress with their wide potential applications in optoelectronics and in biomedical field. GQD have possess interesting properties such as ultra-small size, tunable photoluminescence, excellent aqueous solubility, good physical / chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity (Song et al., 2015; Hill and Galan, 2017; Fang et al., 2017). GQD have many potential applications such as fluorescent dye, photo-catalyst agent, biosensors, bioimaging, drug carriers, gene delivery, therapeutic diagnosis and photo-stimulated therapy (Tian et al., 2018). Nano size and presence of surface defect GQD produce oxidative stress, thus result in increases reactive oxygen species (ROS), which further induce oxidative DNA cleavage, have potential in anticancer therapy (Liu et al., 2011; Li et al., 2013). Due to nanosized of GQD, ROS are producing which induce oxidative DNA cleavage and hence have potential in anticancer therapy (Tian et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2011).

Characteristic examples of carbon dots are graphene quantum dots (GQD) and carbon nanodots (CND), which are further classified into carbon nanoparticles (CNP), carbon quantum dots (CQD) and polymer carbon dots (PCD). In general, GQD and CQD possess obvious lattice structure, while CNP and PCD are amorphous (Zhou et al., 2012). GQD, as sp2 -hybrid single layer nanocrystals, are highly fluorescent in solution or/and film state, even in living cell, due to their quantum confinement and edge effect properties (Han et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2013). The magnetic property of GQD due to their spin polarized edge state, has potential application in the optoelectrical devices. Photoluminescence property due to the quantum confinement effect, delocalization of π-bond electron upon excitation. Absorption of multiple longer wavelengths of photon, small size GQD emit shorter wavelength due increases of the band gap opening energy (Eg) (Tian et al., 2018; Zhang and Yu, 2016; Nurunnabi et al., 2015).

GQD, a 2-dimensional single atom, thick honeycomb structure of carbon-based nano-material, were first serendipitously discovered and reported during “the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes through preparative electrophoresis in 2004 and then laser ablation of graphite in 2006” (Hill and Galan, 2017; Sun et al., 2017; Kan et al., 2009; Hu et al., 2017). The synthetic approaches employed for production of GQD can be classified into two categories: (i) Top-down and (ii) Bottom-up (Fang et al., 2017). Top-down approach, as the term infers cutting of bulk carbon sources and carbonizing into smaller nanomaterials, including carving of graphite crystalline by laser ablation, electron beam lithography, hydrothermal cutting, electrophoretic oxidation and chemical oxidation. This process usually grieves from some drawbacks, such as the requirement of specific equipment, critical synthetic conditions, low quantum yield and difficulty in controlling the size distribution of the particles (Hu et al., 2017). Bottom-up strategy, refers to carbonizing of specific organic precursors into small nano-size nanoparticles, such as carbohydrates, citric acid, fruit juices, milk, honey, plant leaves, cotton, coconut shell and husk, gelatin and urea, using various methods like thermal treatment, microwave assisted hydrothermal treatment, acid pyrolysis, carbonization, direct nucleation and polymerization etc. The bottom-up approach usually allows precise control over the shape, size distribution, and physiochemical properties of the particles (Xu et al., 2004).

Hydrothermal approach has a potential influence on the synthesis of GQD, where water is used as a green solvent and has important role to play in economic atom reaction. The process of hydrothermal deoxidation to reduce graphene oxides and carbon precursors by acidic and alkaline solvent introduces carbonyl and epoxy functional groups on the carbon lattice (Xu et al., 2013; Tabish and Zhang, 2016). Pyrolysis or carbonization of organic carbon precursor by direct heating above their melting point causes condensation, aromatic nucleation, polymerization forms a narrow size distribution of GQD. Precursors includes organic salts such as ascorbic acid, citric acid, glycerol, EDTA, l-glutamic acid, glucose, coffee beans, glucosamine and xylitol etc. (Zheng et al., 2015).

Bovine serum albumin (BSA) is multifunctional protein that acts as the carrier and deposition of many exogenous and endogenous macromolecules including amino acids, fatty acids, metal ions and numerous pharmaceuticals by means of hydrophobic, electrostatic and metal interactions hydrogen bonding (Ghosh et al., 2016). It has proven to be an effective tool for protein-drug interaction studies, due to its specific binding with drugs, macromolecules and dyes etc. makes it suitable candidate for drug delivery applications(Wani et al., 2018). BSA itself is a fluorescent protein molecule which contains two tryptophan residues that have intrinsic domains. (i) Trp134, which is situated on the surface of subdomain IB and (ii) Trp212, locating inside the hydrophobic binding pocket of subdomain IA (Wani et al., 2020). The binding sites of BSA for various exogenous and endogenous macromolecule may be in these domains (Alanazi et al., 2019). The fluorescence quenching as a result of the interactions can switch in the microenvironment inside the domains. The interaction of these domains with graphene could avoid fluorescence quenching (Zargar et al., 2020). The study and understanding of binding interaction between BSA and GQD at the molecular level is therefore crucial in evaluating the biological activity (Tan et al., 2013). The interaction intensity between antitumor drugs and serum albumin may affect their toxicity and bioavailability (Al-Mehizia et al., 2019).

Here we described the carbodiimide activated amidation process for functionalization of GQD with protein, which comprises two steps. In 1st step, carboxylic functional groups on GQD were activated by N-ethyl-N’-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcabodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and stable active ester was formed in the presence of N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS) (Shen et al., 2011). In 2nd step, the active ester was reacted with amine groups on BSA to form an amide bond between GQD and BSA. Thus, resulted in definite uniform attachment of BSA on GQD and caused no denaturing of the protein species (Shen et al., 2010).

The prime inspiration of the present work was to develop GQD by simple, efficient and low-cost fabrication by hydrothermal treatment and pyrolysis of citric acid at relatively low temperature through a less time-consuming process. The obtained GQD were then conjugated with BSA through Carbodiimide activated amidation reaction. The prepared GQD-BSA complex was characterized by various physicochemical methods and which could be employed in the future as a fluorescent probe for bio-imaging and targeting of tumor tissues.

Citric acid monohydrate, and quinine sulfate were purchased from CDH, Mumbai. NaOH pellets were purchased from SRL, Mumbai. Agarose for gel-electrophoresis, Ethidium bromide (EtBr), Bovine serum albumin (BSA), Dialysis membrane (MWCO, 10−12 kDa), were purchased from HI media, India. Polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane syringe filters 0.22 and 0.45 μm were purchased from Axiva, Delhi. N-ethyl-N’-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcabodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) was purchased from TCI, Mumbai. N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Mumbai. Coral Load PCR dye was obtained from Qiagen, Japan. Tris-acetate-EDTA (TEA) buffer and prepared TLC plates were purchased from Merck, Mumbai. Methyl blue, Methyl orange, Coomassie brilliant blue, Trypan blue were used as received.

Different approaches were employed for synthesis of GQDs. In first hydrothermal method, 1.0 gm of Citric acid (CA) was dissolved in 10 mL of water in air-tight glass tube. Then glass tube was placed in stainless steel autoclave and held at 150℃ for 2 h and the solution was then allowed to cool at room temperature. The product was centrifuged at 5000 RPM for 20 min to separate the agglomerated larger particles, if any. The supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm PVDF membrane syringe filter for further characterization (Fang et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2017). In second synthesis method, direct pyrolysis of 1.0 gm of CA was kept in 10 mL screw cap container and heated at 200℃ using heating mantle. After 5 min, the mixture was liquified. After 10 min, the color of the liquid changed from colorless to pale-yellow to orange to brown, indicating the formation of GQD. The obtained solution was neutralized to pH 7.0 by pouring dropwise into 10 mL 0.1 N NaOH solution under vigorous stirring. The aqueous solution of GQD was centrifuged at 5000 RPM for 20 min to separate the larger particles and then filtered by 0.22 μm PVDF membrane syringe filter for further characterization (Hill and Galan, 2017; Agapito et al., 2010; Liang et al., 2013; Wu et al., 2013; Chandra et al., 2012).

Briefly, 8 mg of EDC and 12 mg of NHS were added into 10 mL GQD solution. The solution was taken and sealed in glass container and autoclaved at 150℃ for 2 h, and second reaction was carried out at 100℃ for 2 h. under constant stirring. Then all reaction solutions were allowed to cool to room temperature. Finally, 1.0 mL of 10 % w/v BSA solution was added to this solution and the resulting mixture was incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. The removal of residual unreacted BSA and EDC/NHS from the GQD, the solution was dialyzed through dialysis membrane (MWCO, 10−12 kDa) in distilled water for 24 h (Dialysate was replaced every 6 h). The purified GQD-BSA conjugates were stored at 4℃ for further characterization (Shen et al., 2010, 2011).

The nano-morphology and shape of GQD was recorded by Multimode-8 AFM (Bruker scientific, USA). A glass piece of 1 cm2 area containing one drop of dried GQD suspension was made prior to the analysis. The cantilever was loaded onto the AFM holder and the sample mounted on microscope stage and distance was adjusted. The position of the laser and detector were maneuvered onto the tip of the cantilever. Adjust an integral gain and minimum noise with fine tune before scanning for better tracking, and analyzed particle size by plane-fitting and flattening. Then convert graph in 3D surface images and make histogram by dragging vertical and/or horizontal line (Zhang et al., 2012).

The TEM images of GQD were recorded by Tecnai 20 S-TWIN (Philips, Netherland), system operating at 200 kV of accelerating voltage. TEM samples were prepared by putting a drop of GQD solution on copper grid and then placed onto the sample holder, adjust the objective aperture and selected optimum area to enhance the contrast by blocking electron diffraction at high angle (Zhang et al., 2012). Zoom in and focus into the selected area by gradual increasing and made the resolution up to 50 nm. An image was generated by interaction of electrons transmitted through sample and then images was guided to the imaging device, sensors and detector.

The FTIR spectra of GQD and conjugated GQD were obtained by spectrophotometer FT/IR-6100 (Jasco, Japan) using resolution of 4 cm−1. Analysis was carried out by directly placing a drop of liquid onto the sample holder and IR spectra recorded in the range of 400−4000 cm−1.

The nano size distribution of GQD nanoparticles solution was performed by Dynamic light scattering (DLS) NanoPartica SZ-100 (Horiba, Japan). Sample cuvette was inserted into the sample holder and allowed to equilibrate. Started run to initiate the correlation function at 90°θ. The size distribution was recorded in mean diameter, Z- average value which indicated the mean fraction diameter of standard deviation of GQD nanoparticles in the medium.
The absorbance of GQD solution was measured by UV spectrophotometer (UV-2450 (Shimadzu, Japan). The cuvette was placed into the sample holder and scanned in the range of 800−200 nm.

All 2-dimensions PL spectra, emission and excitation intensity were obtained with a Spectro-fluorometer FP-6500 (Jasco, Japan). The 3D fluorescence spectra were recorded in 3D spectrum measurement, wavelength interval was adjusted to 10 nm and slit widths of 10 nm for both emission and excitation respectively (Zhou et al., 2012).

“Quantum yield is defined as number of emitted photons alienated by number of absorbed photons.” It depends upon the rate of emission divided by the sum of the rate of all other deactivation process. The photoluminescence of GQD was calculated by using Quinine sulfate (QS) has Φ(QS) about 54.3 % in 0.1 N H2SO4 as a standard, with employing the below equation (Pires et al., 2015):
(1)
Φ
G Q D
=
Φ
Q S
X
I
G Q D
I
Q S
X
A
Q S
A
G Q S
X
η 2
G Q D
η 2
Q S
Where, ΦGQD and ΦQS are represents the quantum yield of the GQD and Quinine sulfate. I represent the integrated emission intensity; A denoted the optical density and η is designated the refractive index of the solvent. The quantum yield (Φ) of the GQD was measured at the 330 nm excitation wavelength. The solution concentration was adjusted to accomplish a minimal absorbance of less than 0.1 (Das et al., 2017).

The protein conjugation with GQD were characterized by Bio-Rad electrophoresis system (USA), 10 μL of protein, conjugates and nanoparticles solution were loaded in wells of 1.2 % agarose gel with different loading dyes e.g., Methyl orange, Coomassie brilliant blue, Coral Load dye and Trypan blue. An electrophoretic run was optimized by applying Volts between 30–90 until half-run and/or complete protein band got separated.

The mechanism of interactions of ligand with receptor/target therapeutic protein was done by in-silico molecular docking programs (preparation of protein binding site and ligand, docking process, structural based virtual screening and post-docking analysis). Accuracy of these docking mechanisms remains intricated due to incomplete understanding of the ligand binding process. Molecular docking software such as AutoDock tool v1.5.6, PyRx v8.0, Discovery Studio Visualizer v19.1.0.18287 and PyMOL v2.3.1 have been used for virtual screening and identification of structural based molecular interactions. The 3D protein co-crystal structure with ligand (PDB: 3v03) was gained from RCSB-PDB website (http://www.rcsb.org/). The obtained structure comprises of ligand and water molecule, which must be removed before docking by using Discovery Studio Visualizer. The 2D structure of GQD used in the docking was prepared by ChemDraw ultra v 12.0.2.1076 software, then converted into 3D structure in format of. SDF and. PDB using Open babel software (Gantala et al., 2013; Gohil and Noolvi, 2020).

The ligand was docked with the proteins active binding site of. PDB file using AutoDock vina of PyRx software. The ligand binding affinity was measured in kcal/mol an unit of docking score. It ranked and provided all possible conformation/orientation of GQD-BSA complex (Trott and Olson, 2009). Interaction and binding of docked ligand with the BSA protein was done by PyMol. H-bond, hydrophobic interactions and binding affinity of docked molecule was examined by Discovery Studio Visualizer.

When a citric acid is heated over melting temperature, it decomposes and form hydronium ions, which act as a catalyst in following degradation reactions. The carbon molecules undergo polymerization reaction followed by aldol condensation led to the formation of soluble polymer. Further cycloaddition of these soluble polymer primes to formation of aromatic clusters. Once concentration of these aromatic clusters rises to a critical supersaturation point, a burst nucleation take place and GQD are produced (Chandra et al., 2012). As shown in Fig. 1 , Characterization of GQD: (a) UV-spectroscopy of GQD, optical absorption of GQD typically exhibited with a tail spreading to the visible range in UV region, due to some absorption shows n-p* transition of CO bonds, and p-p* transition of the CC bonds (Naik et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement and evaluation of fluorescence emission from GQD nano-crystals
ce:section.ce:para: The solution of GQD produces from Citric acid, emitted green fluorescence at 500 nm, when excited with 360 nm blue UV beam as shown in Fig. 1(b), Fluorescence images of GQD. Form Fig. 1(c), Spectro-fluorescence overlay 3D graph, it is observed that an emission was excitation-independent. These emissions inferred that surface state of the sp2 clusters and size of GQD were found uniform. Whereas the maximum emission wavelength is directly depending on the concentration of the GQD in solution, emission intensity increased with the concentration. As shown in Fig. 2 , Fluorescence emission spectra of GQD prepared from citric acid emitted higher luminescence intensity, undergone lower carbonization with particle size less than 20 nm in size and with bright green luminescence were found (Liang et al., 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological characterization and evaluation of GQD by AFM and TEM
ce:section.ce:para: AFM was used to measure the topographical morphology of any nanomaterials found in the orange subfraction within the diagram. AFM images from Fig. 3 , shown that prepared GQD was well dispersed in solution and persist as individual particles. Size distribution histography indicates that the 2D-GQD with minimum lateral size of less than 20 nm in diameter have thickness distributed within range of 1.0–3.5 nm (Liang et al., 2013; Chandra et al., 2012; Selim et al., 2009).

The morphology and size distribution were characterized by TEM without further ultrasonication. As shown in Fig. 4 , TEM image of GQD were quasi-spherical and well dispersed in alkaline 0.1 N NaOH solution with transparent appearance. The hydrophilic nature of GQD produces the lateral size about 6.5 nm. The obtained particle sizes are consequences of solvent stabilization following in the self-assembled motif. This self-assembly was determined by electrostatic interactions of superficial functional groups and solvent (Fang et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2012; Selim et al., 2009).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of particle size distribution of GQD and its BSA complex in solutions
ce:section.ce:para: DLS was used to carry out the particle-size distribution of GQD-BSA conjugates at 25℃. Fig. 5 shown the DLS images of GQD-BSA complex. (a) Citric acid GQD shows similar average diameter about 5 nm. After conjugation, the sizes of GQD-BSA complex increased to about 50 nm as shown in Fig. 5(b) (Wu et al., 2013). It was observed that the size of GQD as measured by TEM and DLS was considerably similar that observed by AFM measurement (Zhou et al., 2012).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of functional groups present in GQD-BSA complex by infrared spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The conjugates of GQD-BSA complex were characterized by FTIR spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 6 (a). There are several distinguishing peaks were observed such as, OH stretching vibration at 3300 cm−1 representative of carboxylic acid, CC stretching vibration from the aromatic skeletal ring of graphitic motif at 1638 cm−1 and Acyl CO stretching at 1212 cm−1. In the spectrum of GQD-BSA complex, new weak band at 1738 cm−1 was observed due to NH bending of amide bonds bridging between GQD and active residues of the BSA protein. (Chandra et al., 2012; Chen et al., 2011; Naik et al., 2017; Ruan et al., 2014; Dif et al., 2009).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of GQD-BSA complex based on fluorescence emission
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6(b) shows the fluorescence overlay spectra of GQD-BSA complex. It was observed that there are changes in fluorescence emission shift after conjugation process. GQD-BSA complex by EDC/ NHS shows double distinct fluorescence emission peaks of GQD and BSA, which confirmed that protein was successfully conjugated with GQD (Wang and Qiu, 2015). The GQD obtained from citric acid have an intense optical emission with 12.7 % quantum yield, because only 40 % particle represents photoluminescence of the composite rather non-luminescence oligomers (Pires et al., 2015).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification and separation of protein/macromolecule by 2D-electrophoretic assay
ce:section.ce:para: We further explored the Agarose gel electrophoresis method as a potential tool for the physical characterization of GQD-BSA complex. Owing to the electrostatic and covalent interaction between hydrophilic groups on the surface of the GQD and BSA, certain detailed bands of protein could be obtained. A number of trials were performed with different loading compositions of prepared complex using different loading dyes e.g., Methyl orange, Coomassie brilliant blue and Trypan blue. But non-obvious band separation was observed in all after half run without any fluorescence band, it is supposed to be due to highly hydrophilic nature of the GQD. Then we confirmed Coral Load dye (Qiagen PCR kit) for direct loading of the complex onto an agarose gel. This contains three marker dyes like orange, pink and yellow that discrete the migration distance of protein and optimization of gel run time. The post electrophoretic run depicted in Fig. 7 , the images of gel electrophoresis of EDC/NHS amidation conjugation showed two separate bands of complex and total gel run time (Ren et al., 2012; Xiao et al., 2016). In conclusion EDC/NHS amidation conjugation was successful in binding of the GQD with BSA.

AutoDock vina of PyRx was easily docked many probable conformation/orientations of the GQD on the active surface of BSA protein. Moreover, it’s provided the score of the 09-best conformation of the GQD. Confirmation of the all the different orientation and visualize binding site of BSA protein has been done by Discovery Studio Visualizer and PyMol Molecular Graphics System. In the outcomes among all the orientation of the GQD, the Zero orientation is the best fitting result as shown in Fig. 8 . Where the receptor binding affinity is -10.2 kcal/mol, it is better than the other docked orientations. The molecular dynamic simulation initiated by the ligand depends on the it’s affinity of the binding with the targeted amino acid residues of the receptor protein. The higher negative value of docking score is a result of the better intermolecular force produces in-between ligand and receptor and/or vice-versa. Hence, the interactions are mostly depending on the physio-chemical properties of the ligand (Marsh, 2011). Table 1 , comparing the binding affinities of the different 09 orientations of docked GQD-BSA complex.

We have observed the different types of binding interaction pattern between GQD and the active site residues (amino acid) of BSA protein in Fig. 9 and Table 2 , here we mainly concentrated on the hydrogen bonding interaction and hydrophobic interaction to measure the GQD-BSA complex interactions (Horňák et al., 1999). The GQD shows the closed covalent hydrophilic interactions with Ala258 and Lys286 amino acid residues. The Ala258 formed singular H-bond polar interaction with -NH2 of GQD is length about 2.18°A, while Lys286 formed two different -C = O bond polar interactions with GQD are length about 2.23°A and 2.55°A. Aside as of traditional interactions, GQD has shown the hydrophobic interactions with the Lys262, Pro282, Leu283 residues of the BSA protein.

Herewith we explicate paradigm of hydrophilic GQD crystals development from carbohydrate cores. When core heated above their melting point, hydronium ions decomposed and catalysis the subsequent degradation reactions. Soluble polymer formed by polymerization of carbon molecules followed by aldol condensation. Further aromatic clusters formed by cycloaddition of these soluble polymers. A burst nucleation occurs when the concentration of aromatic clusters reaches to a critical supersaturation phase and GQD are produced (Chandra et al., 2012).

We explain the correlation between the route of the tunable fluorescence emission with the shape of the crystal formation and size of the quantum particles. Our outcomes indicated that there is a correlation between the fluorescence emission with the particle size (Liang et al., 2013). As the quantum particle size increases, π-π stacking of the electron in the outer orbits resulting extension of the sp2 hybridization domain in the sp3 matrix (Naik et al., 2017). Hence, smaller size GQD emission shifted to red upon absorption of longer wavelength photon, and increasing band gap energy (Eg) (Tian et al., 2018). The maximum size limit of the that fluorescence quantum particle is typically ∼ 10 nm; therefore, synthetic method produces nanoparticles size > 10 nm or weakly fluorescence (Tian et al., 2018; Zheng et al., 2015). Though, the only size factor is not describing the fluorescence, other factors such as presence of the other metals and functional groups, like boron, nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorous, sulfur and their location in carbon matrix could affects the fluorescence and quantum yield. In addition, the presence of defect sites is also correlating with fluorescence emission, the larger number of sp3 carbon and existence of nitrogen, oxygen and phosphorous would enhances the surface defect (Naik et al., 2017).

Characterization of GQD was done by various spectral analyses including TEM, AFM, DLS, FTIR, etc. The developed GQD existed in a single layer with narrow size diameter range of 5–20 nm. Functional groups such as CO, CO, were presented to the surface of the GQD, allowing the conjugation as therapeutic targeting ligands or components for biomedical applications such as bio-imaging, bio-sensors, gene and drug delivery (Liang et al., 2013; Chandra et al., 2012; Selim et al., 2009; Naik et al., 2017).

The augmented reactive oxygen functional groups of GQD could execute as good substrate for an immobilization of biomolecules through covalent bonding. Mostly the chemical reactions take place between the side groups of amino acid residues positioned on the protein surface and functionalized groups available on the GQD surface (Simsikova and Sikola, 2017). Therefore, GQD can be activated before binding to biomolecules, typically by the directly introducing electrophilic groups on the surface, which minimized the leakage issue and enhance the operational stability with high temperature, non-polar organic solvents, pH and longtime storage (Qiu et al., 2015). Covalent immobilization also leads to steric modifications in proteins result in decreases functionalization and enzymatic activity. Though, the appropriate use of the cross-linker reagents can often prevent these changes. The cross-linker molecule tie together with GQD surface by non-covalent functionalization such as π-π staking and hydrophobic interaction and covalent binding together with proteins through amide bonds (Zhang et al., 2013). Here we reported a very simple conjugation approach for functionalization of GQD with Bovine serum albumin through carbodiimide-activated amidation. Based on spectroscopically interpretation we observed that the aromatic group of carbodiimide-succinimdyl ester as a cross-linker interacted strongly with basal surface of graphene through π-π stacking, thus well controlled micropattern of EDC/NHS could be created on graphene surface (Shen et al., 2011). The reactive succinimide ester groups of -NH2 could bind exactly with the −COOH functional group of BSA (Simsikova and Sikola, 2017). FT-IR data reveals the formation of amide bridge between the −COOH and -NH2 functional groups of GQD and BSA vice-versa (Chandra et al., 2012; Qiu et al., 2015). Spectro-fluorescence data of GQD-BSA complex shows the two intensive emission peaks after complex formation and 2D-electrophoresis result reveals the credibility and stability of GQD-BSA complex (Qiu et al., 2015) This whole process is easy, scalable and industrial compatible. Anyhow, this process will cause no denaturation of proteins, ensure the uniform attachment of biomolecules and promising to be utilize in future for bridging other biological molecules with graphene-based composite materials (Shen et al., 2010, 2011).

The ligand binding interactions derived from in-silico molecular dynamic simulation and virtual screening framework are often involves in biological reactions essential for receptor-ligand binding. We have established a structure based virtual screening framework by PyRx and performed post-screening analysis through PyMOL and Discovery Studio Visualizer (Trott and Olson, 2009). The least negative binding energy of ligand in contrast to target protein is considered as “binding affinity”, which makes it possible to understand how the ligand interact with the receptor. The docking status was accomplished best binding capacity of GQD against BSA with binding affinity -10.2 kcal/mol. The length of H-bond polar interaction in GQD-BSA complex was found in range of 2.18°A to 2.55°A (Marsh, 2011; Horňák et al., 1999). We believe that these findings are useful to understand the GQD-BSA interaction mechanism.

In conclusion, we developed a facile one pot synthesis method to obtain hydrophilic fluorescent 2D-GQD from citric acid through hydrothermal and direct pyrolysis methods which were quite simple, inexpensive and, scalable. DLS, TEM and AFM analysis of prepared GQD has been carried out, these reports have been suggested that the approximate diameter of GQD in less than 10 nm, and shown narrow size distribution pattern in solution. The spectro-fluorescence data reveals that GQD has excitation dependent fluorescence emission. FTIR data shown that the functional groups such as CO, CO, were situated to the edge of the GQD. Also, we have described the simple and effective approach to conjugate protein with GQD via covalent bonding such as EDC/NHS amidation conjugation. These conjugates were characterized by DLS, FTIR, Fluorescence spectroscopy and agarose gel electrophoresis techniques. Also, we have developed a structure based virtual screening and molecular docking simulation by AutoDock vina in PyRx, PyMOL, and Discovery Studio Visualizer. Based on different post-dock screening analysis and higher negative energy force incorporated during the interactions, it was observed that GQD-BSA conjugation was formed rapidly and stable. It is also a point worth noticing that a unique planar morphology confirmed the conjugation of graphene with multi-functional proteins and peptides. These conjugation methods can also be used for conjugation of 2D-GQD with other bio-recognizable entities through amine groups e.g., peptides, antibodies, DNA and RNA vectors etc. These GQD-biomolecule conjugates will become potential candidate for future applications in biomedical field including bio-imaging, therapeutic diagnosis, biosensors, biocatalyst, drug and gene delivery, therapeutics marker, tissue engineering and cellular probing.

Vimal Patel: Conceptualization, Data Curation, Investigation, Formal Analysis, Methodology and Writing-Original Draft Preparation; Jigar Shah: Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology and Writing-Original Draft Preparation; Ajaykumar Gupta: Methodology, Writing-Review and Editing. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sector.
All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this article.
The datasets used and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.
VP & JS conceptualization, data curation, analysis, methodology and writing-original draft preparation; AG methodology, writing-review and editing. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge Dr. Alex George; Executive Vice President, R&D center, Dr. Reddy, Hyderabad. And Dr. Vijay Thiruvenkatam; Faculty of Biological Engineering and Physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Gandhinagar, Gujarat for their unconditional technical support.
